statement.markdown:
Write a function that sorts an array of integers in ascending order. The input array may contain duplicate numbers. Your function should sort the array in-place (i.e., without creating a new array) and return the sorted array.

For example, if the input array is `[5, 3, 8, 2, 5]`, your function should return `[2, 3, 5, 5, 8]`.

You can assume that the input array will not be null or empty.
statement.notes: The function should sort the array in-place.

constraints.description: The length of the input array

examples.explanation: Input array is sorted in ascending order
Input array is sorted in ascending order

hints.text: Use the built-in sort method of the array.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The sort method uses a comparison function to determine the sort order. If the comparison function returns a value less than 0, the elements are in the correct order. If the comparison function returns a value greater than 0, the elements are in the wrong order.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve using a nested for loop to compare each pair of elements and swap them if they are in the wrong order.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses the built-in sort method with a comparison function that subtracts the values of the two elements.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the comparison function returns the correct value for each pair of elements.
commonMistakes: Not using the in-place sort method
